preamble: The shift from the postalveolar fricative [ʃ] to the alveolar stop [t] is a form of consonant hardening known as stopping. It involves moving from a continuous airflow to a complete blockage. While rare as a diachronic law in adult speech, it is a hallmark of child language development.

examples.note: Developmental stopping: substituting a stop for a hushing fricative.
Merger of several historical fricatives into the alveolar stop in specific environments.
